data.title: Deckers Outdoor tops FQ1 estimates and adds buyback firepower, but profit guidance weighs on shares
data.description:
Deckers Outdoor Corporation (NYSE:DECK) slid in postmarket trading on Thursday after reporting sales rose 6.5% in FQ4 to $1.03 billion. On a constant currency basis, sales increased by 7.5% during the quarter.

Domestic net sales were flat during the quarter at $611.6 million, and international sales increased 19.9% to $374.1 million. Sales from the direct-to-consumer channel decreased 1.2% to $410.2 million.

Brand sales growth: UGG +3.6% to $374.3 million, HOKA +10.0% to $586.1 million, Other brands -6.3% to $61.3 million.

The California-based company said gross margin rose to 56.7% of sales from 56.3% a year ago. Operating income was $173.9 million, compared to $144.3 million a year ago. EPS was reported at $1.00 vs. $0.61 consensus and $0.82 a year ago.

CEO update: "While the global trade environment has introduced greater near-term uncertainty, we are very confident in the exciting opportunities ahead for HOKA and UGG. We view these brands as industry leaders, each with iconic and innovative products that operate in differentiated marketplaces. Alongside Deckers’ superb balance sheet, this positions us well to manage through the near-term with a focus on the long-term."

During FQ4, the company repurchased approximately 1.78 million shares of its common stock for a total of $266.0 million at a weighted average price paid per share of $149.62. Deckers (NYSE:DECK) ended the quarter with an inventory position of $495.2 million, vs. $474.3 million a year ago.

Looking ahead, Deckers (DECK) guided for FQ1 sales to be in a range of $890 million to $910 million and EPS of $0.62 to $0.67  (midpoint $0.645) vs. $0.79 consensus. On the capital allocation front, the board approved an increase of $2.25 billion to the company's stock repurchase authorization, which brings its total outstanding authorization for buybacks to approximately $2.5 billion.

Deckers Brands (DECK) also announced the appointment of Cindy Davis as Chair of the Board of Directors, effective immediately. She succeeds Mike Devine, III, who is retiring from the board after over 14 years of service, including six years as Chair. Davis joined the Board in 2018 and has served as Chair of its Talent & Compensation Committee since 2019 and as a member of its Corporate Responsibility, Sustainability & Governance Committee since 2021. 

Shares of Deckers Outdoor (DECK) fell 10.9% in the postmarket session on Thursday, after adding 2.8% during the regular session. The footwear stock is down almost 45% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Cavco Non-GAAP EPS of $5.40 beats by $0.60, revenue of $508.36M beats by $4.21M
data.description:
• Cavco press release (NASDAQ:CVCO): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $5.40 beats by $0.60.

data.title: Safran sees Boeing regaining momentum on 737 Max production
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) is showing signs of renewed production momentum on its 737 Max program following years of turbulence, Olivier Andries, chief executive of French aerospace group Safran (OTCPK:SAFRY) (OTCPK:SAFRF), a major supplier to the U.S. planemaker, said Thursday, according to a report by Reuters.

Speaking at Safran’s annual shareholder meeting, Andries said Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) production pace on the single-aisle 737 Max has reached nearly 38 jets a month, the current maximum allowed by U.S. aviation regulators following a mid-air panel failure on an Alaska Airlines flight last year.

Boeing Commercial Airplanes’ vice president of quality, Doug Ackerman, also said earlier this week that the company aims to stabilize monthly output at 38 aircraft over the coming months.

Safran (OTCPK:SAFRY) (OTCPK:SAFRF), which jointly builds the CFM International LEAP engine with GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE), supplies powerplants for all Boeing (BA) 737 Max aircraft. The LEAP also competes with Pratt & Whitney on the Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) A320neo platform.

The Safran chief also noted that the company is benefiting from increased demand for aftermarket engine services, a trend he attributed partly to ongoing delays in new aircraft deliveries amid ongoing supply chain challenges across the aerospace industry.

data.title: Codere Online receives Nasdaq delisting notice
data.description:
• Codere Online (NASDAQ:CDRO) received a Nasdaq delisting notice on May 16, 2025, due to its failure to file its 2024 Form 20-F on time.
  • The filing delay stems from the prolonged audit process following the appointment of a new independent auditor on December 31, 2024.
  • Codere filed its delayed 2023 Form 20-F on May 1, 2025, and is working to file the 2024 report by May 30, 2025.
  • CDRO has formally requested a hearing to appeal the delisting and a further stay of trading suspension during the review.
  • If Nasdaq denies the trading stay, Codere’s securities will be suspended on June 6, 2025.
  • Source: Press release

data.title: Magyar Bancorp announces buyback program for up to 5% of outstanding shares
data.description:
Magyar Bancorp (NASDAQ:MGYR) announced on Thursday a stock repurchase program to buy back up to 5% of the community bank's outstanding shares.

The total number of shares set to be repurchased is expected to be up to 323,547.

The company said the repurchased shares will be held as treasury stock and will be available for general corporate purposes.

Additionally, Magyar said it has completed its previously announced buyback program, having repurchased 337,146 shares at an average price of $12.23.

data.title: BBVA Argentina outlines revised 2025 loan growth target of 45–50% amid monetary tightening
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Banco BBVA Argentina S.A. (BBAR) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Belén Fourcade highlighted Argentina's macroeconomic moderation, noting "notable fiscal consolidation, monetary stringency and relative exchange rate stability have contributed to a moderation process of inflation throughout 2024, which has continued at the beginning of 2025." She added that BBVA Research forecasts economic activity to expand by around 5.5% in 2025 with inflation converging to approximately 35%.
  • Fourcade introduced the BBVA Group's new global strategy for 2025–2029, focused on "a radical customer-centric perspective; value and capital generation, and growth in a changing environment; and leveraging accelerators such as artificial intelligence for efficient data processing."
  • Digitalization initiatives have been pivotal, with "new customer acquisition through digital channels reached 86% versus 81% a year ago" and "retail digital sales measured in units reached 93% in the first quarter of 2025 and represent 86% of the bank's total sales measured in monetary value."
  • BBVA Argentina's inflation adjusted net income in Q1 2025 was ARS81.6 billion, a 16.2% increase quarter over quarter, driving a quarterly ROE of 11.5% and ROA of 2%.
  • Fourcade stated, "the 56.9% increase in quarterly operating results was explained by higher income and lower operating expenses."

Outlook

  • Diego Cesarini explained that loan growth guidance for 2025 has been revised downward: "we're thinking about growth in real terms of around 45% to 50% for the year, that is in line with our 11% growth in the first quarter. Regarding deposits, we are also revising downwards, we are seeing around 25% in real terms and we are still keeping our ROE guidance between the mid-teens to biased to low teens."
  • Cesarini indicated capital projections have improved: "we are now forecasting by December a ratio around -- between 16% and 16.5%."
  • The rationale for more conservative growth guidance is linked to monetary policy: "liquidity could be a little concerned this year as the government is keeping a very restrictive monetary policy... we are being a little more conservative on growth just for that."

Financial Results

  • Net interest income totaled ARS541.3 billion, up 3.3% quarter over quarter, with the increase attributed to better performance in fee income and reduced interest expenses.
  • Net income reached ARS99.8 billion, up 48.3% from the previous quarter, while fee income rose to ARS180.1 billion, a 20.7% quarter-over-quarter increase, driven by credit card fees and loan-related income.
  • Loan loss allowances increased 4.9% due to real growth in the loan book.
  • Operating expenses were ARS423.8 billion, down 13.8% quarter over quarter, largely driven by a 23% decrease in personnel benefits following high severance costs in Q4 2024.
  • Private loans rose to ARS9.2 trillion, an 11.2% quarter-over-quarter increase, with notable growth in consumer loans, overdrafts, and mortgages.
  • Consolidated market share of private sector loans reached 11.28%.
  • Asset quality ratio was 1.38%, rising slightly due to seasonal credit card arrears.
  • Capital ratio was 21.5%, with capital excess at ARS1.5 trillion, supported by a regulatory change aligning risk requirements with Basel IV.

Q&A

  • Brian Flores, Citi, asked about updated guidance and capital expectations. Cesarini responded with revised targets for loan growth (45–50%), deposits (25%), and ROE (mid to low teens), and a capital ratio forecast of 16–16.5%. Flores requested clarification on the reasons for more conservative targets, to which Cesarini cited continued restrictive monetary policy and potential liquidity concerns but maintained confidence in short-term growth.
  • Pedro Leduc, Itau BBA, questioned the sustainability of declining SG&A and NIMs. Cesarini explained the NIMs drop was less pronounced despite rate cuts, attributing this to changes in asset mix and inflation dynamics, and expects only a "soft decrease" moving forward. Fourcade noted that the reduction in personnel expenses was mainly due to one-off severance costs in Q4 2024, with administrative expense declines partly due to tax reclassifications.
  • Carlos Gomez, HSBC, asked about dollar loan demand and economic assumptions. Cesarini indicated that recent strong dollar lending was demand-driven, but growth would likely moderate due to funding constraints. He stated BBVA's economic assumptions are 5.5% GDP growth, 35% inflation, and an exchange rate "a little below ARS1,400" for 2025.
  • Jorge Mauro, Fundamenta, inquired about credit card loan growth potential. Cesarini acknowledged other loan products are now growing faster due to shifting customer preferences, but sees further growth in credit cards above inflation, while mortgages and long-term loans are expected to gain greater relevance.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, with questions focused on sustainability of growth, profitability, and the impact of regulatory and monetary changes.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone in prepared remarks, shifting to a more measured and cautious stance in Q&A, especially regarding growth forecasts and liquidity risks.
  • Compared to last quarter, both analysts and management acknowledged a more cautious outlook, with management reducing growth projections and signaling increased conservatism due to external monetary policy factors.
  • Direct quotes reflecting management caution included "we are not certain when this policy will change. So, we are being a little more conservative on growth just for that."

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Growth guidance for loans and deposits has been revised downward from last quarter's expectations of 60–65% and 40% real growth, respectively, to 45–50% and 25% for 2025.
  • ROE guidance remains mid to low teens, consistent with the previous quarter, but with an explicit downward bias.
  • Management's confidence has moderated, with more emphasis on liquidity constraints and policy uncertainty.
  • Analysts' questions have shifted from growth and profitability potential to the drivers behind more conservative projections and capital planning.
  • Compared to Q4 2024, asset quality remains strong but there is greater attention to loan mix and funding structure.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited a restrictive monetary policy and possible liquidity constraints as primary reasons for revising growth guidance downward.
  • Dollar-denominated loan growth is expected to slow, dependent on funding conditions and government policy announcements.
  • Analysts expressed concern about the sustainability of expense reductions and NIM stabilization, as well as the structural ability to support long-term mortgage growth without new funding mechanisms.

Final Takeaway

BBVA Argentina’s management is recalibrating its growth strategy in light of continued monetary tightening and evolving market conditions, now targeting 45–50% real loan growth and 25% deposit growth for 2025 with capital and profitability outlooks tempered by liquidity caution. Digital adoption and product diversification remain central, while the bank maintains strong solvency and asset quality. Management signals a more conservative approach, responsive to external policy shifts and funding realities, as it navigates a dynamic macroeconomic environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. ambassador presses NATO for plan toward 5% defense spending goal
data.description:
U.S. Ambassador to NATO Matthew Whitaker is urging alliance members to outline a clear path toward dedicating 5% of their economic output to defense, with a strong emphasis on traditional military capabilities. His comments come as the bloc works to shape a new agreement that aligns with President Donald Trump’s expectations ahead of next month’s NATO summit.

Speaking to Bloomberg Television on Thursday, Whitaker stressed the need for a more ambitious and specific roadmap than the 2% GDP pledge adopted at NATO’s 2014 Wales summit.

“We need to have clear stair steps to meet those goals,” Whitaker said, adding that the bulk of future investments must go toward what he termed “hard defense,” or combat-ready forces, equipment and infrastructure.

The call for increased spending follows continued pressure from Trump, who has made defense contributions a central issue since his first term. Earlier this month, Whitaker signaled that the president may skip the upcoming NATO meeting in The Hague unless member nations formalize a commitment to boost their budgets.

NATO leaders are expected to approve an updated spending target at the summit. One proposal under consideration involves allocating 3.5% of GDP to direct military spending, with another 1.5% going toward related areas, such as defense research and strategic infrastructure, by the year 2032.

The ongoing conflict in Ukraine remains a key driver behind the renewed urgency. Following a recent phone call between Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin—which reportedly ended without a breakthrough—European allies are accelerating their own military investments.

German Chancellor Friedrich Merz responded by stating that Germany is actively increasing its defense budget. Meanwhile, the Netherlands estimated it may need to allocate up to $21.4 billion annually to meet the proposed NATO thresholds.
